experiments.title: How popularity of an article influenced visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will combining relevancy with popularity increase the engagement of visitors on the blog?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2017 - 03/22/2017
For Alliance Defending Freedom, we had previously run experiments that found more relevant blog posts (those in the same content category) were less influential at getting visitors to explore the blog than the most recent posts. We also found that the most popular articles could slightly increase the percentage of people that read another article compared to the most recent posts. We had also found that the tactic used to display the articles (sidebar compared to a list at the bottom) did not have any impact on increasing engagement.
However, in the continued efforts to increase reader engagement, we wanted to see if we could radically alter the visitor experience by combining all three of these (relevancy, popularity, and design). We decided to launch a three-way test targeting desktop visitors to the blog. We would show them the standard control which was the most recent articles and then two versions of the most popular articles based upon the category of the post they were reading.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In both cases, the most popular and relevant content (regardless of where it was displayed) decreased the percentage of people that explored the website. Due to the differences in the treatments, this may have been the result of differing factors.
In the case of the sidebar, our hypothesis is that visitors ignored that area of the website due to “banner blindness.” In the case of the content at the bottom, it is our hypothesis the drop in engagement may be due to the lack of imagery. We wanted to emphasize the popularity so we used the space normally reserved for imagery to show the posts’ position in popularity. This may have decreased the appeal of the article.

experiments.title: How an ‘Up Next’ Slideout Impacts Mobile Clickthrough Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will providing a single article that is “Up Next” increase clickthrough rate on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2017 - 06/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom noticed that their most engaged users–and consequently, the ones that were most likely to become supporters–primarily spent their time reading Alliance Defending Freedom’s blogs. Noticing this as a major step in the donor journey, they decided to test a ‘Up Next’ slideout designed to keep the site visitor engaged with a new article immediately after finishing one. If they could keep their visitor engaged just a little bit longer, they knew they could increase that user’s affinity for their mission–and perhaps, their willingness to support Alliance Defending Freedom. They set up an A/B test to test this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings: Users that were motivated enough to finish reading the entire blog article responded favorably to the ‘Up Next’ slideout. Over the course of two weeks, Alliance Defending Freedom noticed that the treatment did, in fact, increase engagement by nearly 30%. It’s evident that readers wanted more to get more content. By removing the friction of searching for the next blog to read or deciding which topic to choose, Alliance Defending Freedom found that visitors stayed engaged for a longer period of time, especially when the next article was chosen for them. This finding has prompted further side-by-side funnel testing to determine what kind of funnel is the most effective for user engagement. There is also room for similar tests measuring results all the way through the funnel, to view the effects of email acquisition and donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How Mission-Specific Content Impacts Clickthrough Rate on a Mobile Slideout
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase mobile traffic to the ‘Issues’ section of the site without decreasing ‘next article’ clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 08/25/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom found that the ‘Issues’ slideout increased traffic to the ‘Issues’ portion of the site by 49%. We also found that we were able to direct this traffic without negatively impacting the number of visitors that explored the website or read another article.
This test confirms our hypothesis that we can direct traffic to a desired portion of a site, without decreasing ‘next article’ clickthrough rate. In other words, this test proves that we can funnel visitors directly to the organization’s stated mission and immediately bring clarity to the organization’s value proposition without disrupting their online experience. This test has prompted further desktop-only tests, as well as similar site-specific slideout experiments.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom knows that site visitors who spend more time in the ‘Issues’ section of their site have a better understanding of their mission. Earlier this year, they tested into using a ‘Up Next’ slideout on their blog pages. They wanted to determine if they could direct more traffic to the ‘Issues’ section of their site without decreasing clickthrough rate on their mobile slideout. To test run this test, they created an ‘Issues’ slideout as a treatment to see if they could effectively redirect traffic on mobile browsers.

experiments.title: How a direct ask on a blog impacts long-term donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can a direct donation ask on the blog have a significant impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2017 - 05/16/2017
For Alliance Defending Freedom, the mobile version of their blog received a significant volume of traffic from Facebook. Historically, we had used this as an opportunity to acquire new email addresses to the file. We decided to test whether this audience could also have the potential to be a good donor acquisition source. To do this, we replaced the standard email acquisition offer with a direct ask for one of their clients, Barronelle.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the visitors that saw the direct ask ad, were 81% more likely to make a gift than those that did not. At the same time, removing the email offer, reduced the email acquisition by 98%. This reinforced our expected outcome and gives us the knowledge that there are multiple levers we can pull depending upon our desired outcome.
Upon further analysis, we found that the majority of visitors did not make their gift immediately when they saw the direct ask ad. Instead, they came back to that donation page when prompted on a different channel and made their gift at a later date. This would indicate that the blog may be better used reinforcing the need rather than making a direct ask.

experiments.title: How removing the footer on a donation page affects the conversation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Removing the footer on a donation page will achieve an increase in the conversion rate because people won’t get distracted by the many page options to visit and allow them to focus on the purpose of the page–to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2025 - 03/18/2025
Alliance Defending Freedom is one of the leading Christian law firms committed to protecting religious freedom, free speech, marriage and family, parental rights, and the sanctity of life.
We ran this test on the donation page for one of their grant programs. Traffic came to this page through house file email.
We want to see if removing the footer would stop people from bouncing off the donation page and increase the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the footer on the donation page led to a significant increase in transactions for all traffic. The 70.9% increase in conversions shows that the hypothesis that removing the footer would increase the conversion rate was supported by the data.

However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered fully valid. Further testing would be necessary to confirm that removing the footer consistently leads to higher conversion rates.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of removing the footer on different pages or across different campaigns to see if this trend holds true. Additionally, it would be important to consider other elements on the page that may impact conversion rates and test those as well to optimize donation pages further.

experiments.title: How a membership offer on an instant donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a membership donation ask outperform the generic donation traditionally used?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2019 - 02/10/2019
As part of the efforts to validate the potential for a membership program within Alliance Defending Freedom, we identified three of the highest donor-motivated traffic portions of the website. One of these areas was the donation page shown after a visitors signs the “Statement of Belief” found on the blog and on the homepage. For this page, we then created a treatment that introduced the concept of membership and asked for a gift to support the “broad and far-reaching” work being done.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not reach a statistical level of significance with this experiment. Directionally, the membership concept seemed to produce a 36% decrease in donations directly to the campaign but this is something that needs to be further validated with additional testing.

experiments.title: How leading with a question impact email click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does leading an email with a question increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2019 - 07/23/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom was sending out one of their weekly cultivation emails as part of a longitudinal experiment to improve email engagement. In the previous week, we had observed a surprising result in an experiment. When starting the email with a personal anecdote, we saw a decreased level of engagement. We had a hypothesis that this may be due to the way the two emails differed in their introductions. The control started with the question to the reader while the treatment started with the story.
Since there were several other factors that could have led to the observed result, we decided to isolate the question factor and run another A/B split. The only difference in the control and treatments email would then be whether they begin with a question or a statement.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach a statistical level of confidence, we were quickly approaching a validation with a 90% level of confidence. This would suggest that leading with a question is a better way to capture visitor attention that the standard introduction. However, we would need to test this further to validate the concept.

experiments.title: The impact of moving the donation ask earlier in the funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the donation ask earlier in the funnel increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2018 - 06/29/2018
The blog for Alliance Defending Freedom get significant traffic on a daily basis. Over the past several months, we had been able to test into a series of new offers that had lifted the email acquisition rate from less than 1% to 4% – 6%. This meant several hundred new emails were coming on to the file on a weekly basis and each of these subscribers were shown a donation page once they submitted their email. This meant a certain percentage of them would convert to donors instantly.
ADF had recently developed a new embeddable donation form that we wanted to test out on the blog. Historical tests had found that, when linking to a donation form, the blog did not serve as a significant source for donations. We wanted to determine if moving the ability to donate directly on to the blog would allow us to reduce the friction associated with the donation funnel and, thereby, increase donor acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Upon first glance, the embedded donation form appeared to have a 60% decrease to overall conversion. However, when digging deeper into the numbers, we discovered that while there was a 60% decrease between the people that saw the treatment compared to the control, the actual conversions that came from the email acquisition offer compared to the donation offer told a different story. If we looked at just those conversions, the direct donation ask had a 264% increase (at 91% level of confidence).
This variance is the result of the high converting traffic that occurs during the fiscal year end period. Essentially, subscribers would read a blog post and they later got an email asking them to make a donation (which was unrelated to their actions on the blog post). However, since they saw the original blog post, their donation was then attributed to the experiment.
What we can take away from this experiment is that we always need to dig deeper into the top level results reported by the various systems we use. We also now know that we need to re-test this concept once the fiscal year has passed.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action affected a social media audience’s conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding value proposition language to the calls-to-action at the bottom of the page increase the likelihood of a visitor giving their email or making a gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2015 - 11/02/2015
The Alliance Defending Freedom blog receives high volumes of traffic each day, largely from social media. We wanted to find ways to motivate this audience to take one of two actions: either subscribe to the newsletter or make a donation.
In a previous experiment, we discovered that just placing the calls-to-action onto the blog posts wasn’t enough to convince people to take a specific action. For our next iteration of this test, we decided to try testing out adding some value proposition language around the desired actions. We also softened the buttons so that they would communicate value to the visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: This new treatment was able to succeed where the last experiment failed. We were able to acquire email addresses by implementing a name acquisition device on the page with accompanying value proposition language. That said, a 0.04% email conversion rate is certainly not breaking any records but it gives us a new starting point. This will allow us to continue to iterate and improve the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How reducing the value proposition copy affects the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will result in the best email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/07/2015 - 11/15/2015
A previous experiment for Alliance Defending Freedom had found that adding a value proposition, or a reason to give their email, on the homepage was able to increase the number of emails acquired by 44%. With the launch of the new website, we wanted to see if would could decrease the amount of copy required to communicate the same value proposition.
Our hypothesis was that by reducing the copy on the page, we could reduce the amount of mental friction associated with reading it. If it had the same level of value, this reduction in friction would be able to further lift conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered that the shorter value proposition actually reduced the conversion rate by 29.5%. It is our hypothesis that while the treatment was visually shorter, by removing the bullet points, we made it difficult to quickly scan. There was also added value communicated in the headline of the Control treatment with the perceived exclusivity of “Be the first to know”. This tells us that, at least with email acquisition, the added value that comes from the longer value proposition and the additional exclusivity translates into increased conversion.

experiments.title: How a product-level acquisition offer affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer will result in the highest email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2016 - 02/15/2016
In an effort to convert their existing blog traffic, Alliance Defending Freedom has been experimenting with different email acquisition methods and offers. The existing offer promotes a variety of email publications the visitor will receive and unites them under the ADF’s primary value proposition of defending religious freedom.
We had the hypothesis that if we focused the offer around something more tangible, a digital subscription to Faith and Justice Magazine, that the offer would be more appealing to visitors. This same incentive is offered with the current control but it does not describe the details of the magazine or offer a visual.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Faith and Justice offer resulted in a 37% decrease in emails acquired. This would lead us to believe the individual offer of Faith and Justice, while more tangible, was not as attractive of the ultimate goal of the audience which was learning more about their religious freedoms.  The more general offer did a better job of communicating this value proposition.

experiments.title: How clarifying the call-to-action for mobile visitors affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Does including the call-to-action on the homepage banner for mobile devices increase the number of people that explore the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2016 - 04/20/2016
Alliance Defending Freedom has a unique homepage banner that allows for multiple stories, cases, and appeals to be featured. The banner shows the featured image and includes the headline on top of it. When a user hovers over the image, more details of the article appears including the category and a call to action such as “Learn More” or “Double Your Impact.”
While discussing ways to increase the engagement of the visitors to the site, it was realized that mobile users would never see the call-to-action. It is only visible on hover which is a function not available on phones and tablets.  We decided to run a test targeting mobile devices to see if moving the call-to-action below the headline on the image would encourage more users to explore the website.
experiments.keyLearnings: By moving the call-to-action on top of the banner; we were able to increase the number of people exploring the website by 11.5%.  Our hypothesis is that without the call-to-action, the visitors were unclear on what they should do next. It made it more obvious that the banners should be clicked on and it clarified what their next steps would be on the subsequent page.

experiments.title: How adjusting the value proposition language impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition copy will be most appealing (and subsequently have the highest conversion rate) with visitors to the blog?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2016 - 05/02/2016
In an effort to capture more data from visitors to their blog, Alliance Defending Freedom decided to test out a modified version of the existing email offer. Through a series of experiments, we had narrowed in on the active email offer which is a general email opt in that offers news and updates for religious freedom cases. We felt that we could increase the relevance of this offer by highlighting that ADF was truly the “boots on the ground” in these situations and could offer first-hand perspectives, not just an update.
experiments.keyLearnings: The updated value proposition copy led to a 37% increase to the number of emails acquired on the blog. In addition to the increased value that the concept of “first-hand perspectives” brought to the offer, we implemented an element of urgency to the headline. By changing “Get updates on your religious freedom” to “Stay informed on changes to your religious freedom.” we introduced the idea that the “changes” may have (and often do have) a direct impact on their day to day life. This made the email offer more important and personally relevant to the visitors.

experiments.title: How the tone of the call-to-action impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which headline will have the greatest email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2016 - 06/06/2016
Alliance Defending Freedom was launching a campaign in light of the recent controversy caused by the North Carolina bathroom bill. As part of this campaign, the landing page contained a statement of belief that encouraged visitors to submit their name and email.
The initial call-to-action that was found right above the form was “Help Keep Women and Children Safe.” We had the hypothesis that this headline may be unintentionally causing friction in the visitors’ minds. With the way it is worded, it infers that the signer will have to do work by “helping”. The statement below it also did not address how adding their signature would impact women and children.
We decided to run a test with a headline that would more closely align with the copy found below the headline.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new headline led to a 15.6% increase to the number of names and emails acquired. By removing the first word, “Help,” from the headline, we took the emphasis off of what the signer had to do and more closely aligned the headline to the statement of belief that followed it.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action in the email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call to action will drive more traffic which will, in turn, garner more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2014 - 12/19/2014
In the second email of Heritage’s year end campaign, they wanted to find our which messaging stance appeals most to donors and drives the most donations.  They tried three variations: a direct ask, soft call-to-action around the organization, and a soft call-to-action focused on the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The emails with softer calls-to-action were able to increase the number of clicks more than 91%, however, the traffic was not as motivated to give when compared to those who were asked to make a gift within the email.   As a result, the email making a direct ask was able to convert 50% more donors than the other treatments.

experiments.title: How the page layout impacts visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a sidebar impact visitor engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/30/2017 - 02/21/2017
With the ADF blog, we wanted to find new ways to increase overall visitor engagement. Engagement is defined as anything from donations and email acquisition to visitors exploring the site. One way that the blog increased visitors exploring the site was by listing recent articles at the bottom of each post. We had the hypothesis that this may not be seen by a significant volume of visitors if they didn’t finish each post. To overcome this, we decided to test out “sticky” sidebar that would remain on the right side of the page for desktop visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 3 weeks, the sidebar did not produce any significant impact to either emails acquired or visitors exploring the website. The trend seemed to indicate that it may be negatively impacting these metrics so we decided to shut off this experiment in leu of others that we felt may be more impactful.

experiments.title: How a Rotating Exit Intent Offer Impacts Desktop Email Acquisition Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing people a different offer on each visit increase the chances of email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2017 - 06/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom knows that their blog attracts significant amounts of organic traffic. Keeping these visitors engaged is a big opportunity and major step forward in their donor journey. After running a series of tests, they found that using an exit intent pop-up as a final attempt to engage users before leaving a blog page increased email acquisition rate. Though they have seen an increase in engagement and conversion rates since adopting these pop-ups, they wondered if users were weary of seeing the same exit-intent messaging on every visit. To test their hypothesis, they decided to introduce a series of four rotating exit-intent offers. This would provide a new offer on the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th visit.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom noticed that the series of rotating offers appeared to decrease email acquisition rates over the course of one month. However, this test was not statistically valid. Their findings must, consequently, remain anecdotal. This test may suggest that four different offers reduced the familiarity, and therefore the clarity, of the exit intent’s true value proposition–to keep exiting visitor up-to-date on news regarding religious liberty. Further tests will be conducted to discover the answer to this question.

experiments.title: How supporting copy length on a Facebook ad affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the length of the supporting ad copy increase clickthrough rate on a facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2017 - 12/12/2017
Oral arguments for Jack Phillips–a client of Alliance Defending Freedom–began on December 5th. As the news covered this national story, Alliance Defending Freedom decided to launch a Facebook ad campaign urging people to sign a letter of support for Jack. Since they knew some people were unfamiliar with Jack, they wondered: Will increasing the length of supporting ad copy on a facebook ad increase clickthrough rate? To test this, they created a longer treatment and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom that there was no statistical difference in email acquisition between the control and treatment ad. As we look at this test, it’s important to consider the timing of this campaign. Due to the relevant and captivating nature of this material, this campaign experienced a well above-average clickthrough and email acquisition rate. It’s likely that this played a factor in the A/B test (i.e. people were already pretty motivated to clickthrough, so there was less room for incremental optimization).

experiments.title: FY21 – CYE Notecard Image Test
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the image of the notecard for all donors will achieve higher net revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/08/2021
The test was run during December 2020 in the notecard, which landed in-homes around 12/15. The election was a prominent topic in the news at the time, most receiving it just after the electoral college cast their votes. We wanted to see if changing the image on the outer envelope made a difference. Three versions were tested:
1) The control was a stark, wintery image of SCOTUS
2) The first test was an image of a family with young kids walking in a snowy forest
3) The second test was an image of a wintery barn with an American flag
experiments.keyLearnings: First, image selection matters. Second, the image of SCOTUS, which has been our control, was the better route to go. This is likely because our donors care deeply about our work at the Supreme Court and it felt most like ADF.

experiments.title: How a membership offer impacts new visitors to ADF
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “membership” be a more appealing offer than “donating” to new visitors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2019 - 05/13/2019
As part of our efforts to evaluate the impact a membership program could have on the ADF fundraising efforts, we set up an experiment on the Thank You page of an email acquisition campaign. The visitors were coming from the blog after signing a Statement of Belief. We then created a new version of this page with the main modification being the final call to action at the end. We testing the concept of “membership” against the standard “donate” messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this experiment targeted at both new and existing visitors but, when digging into the metrics, we discovered that it had no significant impact on existing visitor conversion but it seemed to directionally impact new visitors likelihood to make a gift. This experiment had run for a full two months and would have required another month before validating so the decision was made to end this experiment to avoid being impacted by the “history effect.”
As next steps, we will roll this experiment out to a dedicated Facebook campaign where we will be able to send more new visitors to the treatment to see if we can replicate this impact in a statistically significant manner.

experiments.title: Messaging/OE Test – July 2014 – KYFAH Appeal I
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a new OE and slightly different appeal outperform the control?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/29/2019
What Was Tested
We changed the Outer Envelope from a photo of Obama, the ACLU, and the Bible with a red X through it which said: What’s behind the unprecedented attacks on your religious freedom … and what you can do about it!  To an OE with a photo of a door that says “Official Notice: Keep your faith at home – or else” with a teaser of: A message to Christians from the ACLU and the Obama Administration. We also varied the text in the appeal slightly to focus more on the ACLU Triple Threat.

experiments.title: How prompting for a reply impacts email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will prompting the reader for a reply increase the email click through rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2019 - 05/21/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom was sending out their weekly email cultivation email to a select group of subscribers. The primary goal of these emails is to increase the overall engagement of this group, as a result we wanted to test out tactics that could improve this metric.
Every email sends them an article to read on the ADF blog. In order to give them a reason to read the blog post, we wanted to try prompting the reader to respond to the email. The belief was that if we gave them a reason to read the article (in this case, to tell us their opinion), they would be more likely to consume the content.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no statistically significant measurable difference between the control and treatment version of this experiment. However, one of the more challenging metrics to measure is the actual replies to this email. This would be a higher form of engagement than a click through rate but it is not easily captured.
This will need to be updated once we receive anecdotal evidence from the ADF team on the number of responses.

experiments.title: How a homepage promotion for the membership program impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Can a promotion for the membership program on the home page increase donor conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2019 - 04/04/2019
After launching a new Membership program, Alliance Defending Freedom wanted to promote the program on their homepage. We wanted to know how the introduction of the membership program overall from the home page would impact donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the home page promotion for over a month, we did notice a slight increase in donor conversion rate of +8.6%, but only achieved a level of confidence of 48.7%, which means that the experiment was not validated.
We did note that within that slight increase in donor conversion lift, that approximately 10% (13/130) gifts did come directly from the home page promotion.
In the end, we collectively agreed that the promotion itself needed a more “fair” treatment for the promotion of the control’s “donate” option.
For that reason, we ended the experiment and will be launching a new treatment which will more “fairly” promote the control’s “donate” path. We will also change the placement (from the right-side of the home page’s “hero image” area, as shown in the screenshot included to just below the “hero image” and the next section of content titled “Latest from the Blog”.

experiments.title: How a vanity URL impacted online giving from a direct mail appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will pushing donors to online giving increase revenue overall?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2019
What Was Tested
We tested adding a box to the bottom of the copy and RD that said: “Give ONLINE at: AllianceDefendingFreedom.org/donate”.
We also added the same message to the CRE.

We saw an insignificant decrease in response rate, which makes me question if these donors went online instead. Average gift and net income were also down.

experiments.title: How an email acquisition offer performs on a ‘Case’ page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing the number of CTAs to a single action and utilizing the Statement of Belief as the offer increase the number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2018 - 07/17/2018
In a recent data analysis, we discovered that ADF’s Case Pages see a significant spike in traffic when a case makes national news. Knowing that the results of the Jack Phillips case and NIFLA would be announced in the coming weeks, we decided to see if we could capitalize on that increase in traffic.
To do that, we created a treatment that added the already highly performing Statement of Belief to the Jack Phillips and NIFLA case pages. It replaced the existing call to actions which were buttons at the bottom for both a newsletter and a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant increase in the number of emails acquired when using the Statement of Belief. Our hypothesis is that the Statement of Belief was not the right offer to best match the motivations of those coming from organic search. Since, for many of them, they were just learning about Jack Phillips and NIFLA for the first time, they were likely not yet ready to sign on to a statement of belief.

experiments.title: How the an individual sender impacted open rate for a cold list
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an individual sender increase the open rate for a prospecting email list?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/16/2018
Alliance Defending Freedom was in the process of cultivating a new list of email prospects with a series of emails. We had noticed a declining open rate so we wanted to see if there was a way to potentially boost this metric. We decided to try sending the emails from an individual (Michael Farris) instead of the organization. We had the hypothesis that people would be more likely to respond to an individual than an organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, we did not see a statistically significant difference between either sender. This is an interesting observation because with previous experiments for ADF related to sender name, we found that individuals will often perform better for their existing housefile. This would suggest that it isn’t just sending from an individual that moves the needle but sending from a known individual.

experiments.title: How email design impacted opens with a prospecting email list
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler email increase the likelihood of the email being opened?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2018 - 05/25/2018
ADF was using their existing email welcome series to “warm up” the purchased prospecting list from Axciom. The open rate of this list was significantly low compared to the standard house file emails so we decided to test some deliverability tactics to improve this metric. We had found the sender did not play a significant roll in increasing the opens so we decided to test the style of the email. The hypothesis was that a simpler email would not be placed in the “Promotions” tab of systems like Gmail and Yahoo which would increase the likelihood of being opened.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant different in open rate between both the designed and plain text emails. This is actually beneficial for ADF as their standard emails to their subscriber base have the more “designed” style which means this email file will fit well within their audience.

experiments.title: How the messaging approach for the donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will targeting the visitors’ fear of loss increase the overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2018 - 05/21/2018
The NIFLA statement of belief had received significant attention in our optimization efforts, especially at the top of the funnel. However, we had not yet done much testing in the final step of the process which was the donation page. This offer was going to be sent to the existing subscriber base so, with the potential influx of visitors, we decided to set up a test on this final page.
After reviewing the existing page, we had a hypothesis that we could improve the results by altering the delivery of the value proposition. Instead of focusing on the work being done, we shifted our message to focus on what could happen if we were to fail. Our hypothesis was that by focusing on people’s fear of loss, we could increase the urgency of the appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, we did not see a statistically significant difference between the two variants. This would suggest the two tactics were not radically different enough to increase the urgency of the appeal. For future testing, it would be best to test a new tactic for increasing the perceived urgency of the appeal.

experiments.title: How the value proposition of the call-to-action impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing on the impact of the gift instead of the challenge aspect increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/26/2018
For their March appeal, Alliance Defending Freedom was sending a follow up email explaining the outcome of their recent Supreme Court case. As part of the appeal, they were also announcing a matching grant and using that as one of the primary reasons to give a gift. Since it was a “challenge” gift, much of the language around the call-to-action was about “accepting the challenge.” We had the hypothesis that by focusing on the value of the gift (ie. to protect lives and freedom) instead of the challenge, we could get more visitors to the website and more gifts to the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no statistically significant impact on overall gifts or clicks between the two treatments. It would appear that the value proposition of the various calls-to-action were not significantly different enough to see a measurable impact.

experiments.title: How modifying the donation pathway impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will linking to the donation widget increase overall donor conversion compared to linking to a separate donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2018 - 03/02/2018
Since the launch of their homepage donation widget (found in the middle of the homepage), Alliance Defending Freedom had seen a significant lift in the amount of revenue that was coming from visitors landing on the homepage. Historically, they had also included a donate link in the banner at the top of the homepage but his button linked to a separate landing page.
We had the hypothesis that linking to the widget may actually cause less friction than linking to a separate page. We decided to test this with one of the monthly appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings: At the conclusion of this experiment, we saw minimal difference between the overall donations to the two variants. When digging into the details, we found that we were able to increase donations to the widget by 72% but this just shifted donations away from the normal donation page; it did not result in net new donations. This just proved that the jump did not eliminate friction within the donation process; it just provides an additional opportunity for giving.

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad’s inline call-to-action impacted results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a call-to-action earlier in the ad increase the number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2018 - 02/27/2018
While running ads for an email acquisition campaign for Alliance Defending Freedom, we wanted to see if we could increase our acquisition by adding a call to action link earlier in the Facebook ad. Normally, a FB ad would only have a button and an image that are linked but it is possible to add another link in the text above if it matches the same location that the other areas go to.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no difference between the two treatments when it comes to the number of emails acquired. We also analyzed the clicks and while the ad with an inline call to action did get a few more visitors, they did not convert on the page. This would indicate that making the call to action available earlier in the process only drove visitors without the proper motivation.

experiments.title: How the method of ad optimization impact offline conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Is it better for donors conversion to get more people to see the brand ads or is it better to get less visibility but more engagement with the ads?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2017 - 12/20/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom wanted to increase the results in their direct mail program during the calendar year end push. One way they did this was by incorporating online brand advertising to the recipients of their direct mail letters. The donors and prospects were shown a series of brand ads that linked to select blog posts on the organization’s website. This methodology was based on past experiments where we found that brand advertising can influence offline behaviors.
For this campaign, we wanted to discover the most impactful method for displaying the ads. In Facebook’s platform, we have the ability to optimize the ad campaign for a handful of different metrics. In this situation, we wanted to know if it was better to optimize for impressions (i.e. get more people to see the ads) or for clicks (i.e. get more people to engage with the ads).
To determine this, we split randomly split the direct mail file and setups two ad campaigns that differed only in how Facebook was optimizing the ad spend.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Once the ads had finished running, we found that we were able to get several hundred more donors and prospects to click on the ad when optimizing for clicks and several thousand more ad impressions when optimizing for that metric. Despite these differences, we found that there was no statistically significant difference in performance between the two optimization methodologies. We also evaluated the segments on revenue and saw the same results.
It would seem that both methods of optimization allowed enough donors to see the ads to have the same impact on conversion.

experiments.title: How high visitor motivation affects donor conversion rate

rows.name: Stand Up Headline with Matching Grant

experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment is going to produce the best conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2015 - 05/31/2015
The standard appeal for Alliance Defending Freedom will begin with an email to their main constituency that contains a strong call-to-action to give a gift. In an effort to increase the conversion rate of the page, we wanted to try several changes add additional value to the potential donors’ gift and reduce friction and anxiety.
We created a treatment that dramatically expanded upon the value proposition being discussed on the page both in the main page copy and in the button at the bottom. We also moved the matching gift incentive to the end of the copy to focus on the primary reasons to give to ADF. We also expanded elements that emphasized the security and financial accountability.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment did not reach significance with either sample size or level of confidence, but we did learn something about the motivation of the donors.
The initial driver of traffic (the email) was crafted in such a way that only the most highly motivated visitors made to the page. We realized that in order to improve the overall response rate of the campaign, we needed to move the optimization efforts back in the donor funnel to the email itself.  If we can increase the number of visitors to the page, we can then impact the overall results of the campaign.

experiments.title: How taking a softer call to action affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call-to-action within the email will drive more traffic to the website which will, in turn, convert a greater number of donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2014 - 12/27/2014
As it was getting closer to the end of the year, Heritage wanted to test a messaging approach with their fifth email in their year end campaign. Toward the end of urgency-based marketing campaigns, it is common practice to make a direct ask in the email.  The question was how early can that direct ask be made and have it still be effective.
experiments.keyLearnings: The softer language in the email increased the number of visitors by 41% but it actually converted fewer total donors in the test.  At this stage in the campaign, there does not seem to be any impact to response rate by utilizing a softer call to action.

experiments.title: How will just adding the calls-to-action affect donations and email acquisition

rows.name: Calls to Action at the Bottom

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the email capture and donation button to the page increase the number of people taking one of those two actions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2015 - 10/28/2015
The Alliance Defending Freedom blog receives high volumes of traffic each day, largely from social media. We wanted to find ways to motivate this audience to take one of two actions: either subscribe to the newsletter or make a donation.
There is a component found on many other areas of the website that gives both of these options but it was not available on the blog. As the first experiment to increase either of these two behaviors, we decided to simply put this widget at the bottom of the page to see if that lifts either the number of donations made or email addresses acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: Just adding the calls to action to the bottom of the screen did not inspire the blog readers to do either of the two actions. In future experiments, we need to find ways to contextualize the calls-to-action so it fits within the content of the blog and we need to emphasize a primary CTA to make the desired action clear to the ready.

experiments.title: How a shift in copy tone affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: An email talking about how Heritage impacts the constituents’ family life will drive more clicks than one talking about how they stand for the constituents’ principles.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2014 - 12/24/2014
In the fourth email in the Heritage year end campaign, we tested what messaging point (principles or family) would drive the most traffic to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the two treatments spoke to different prospect level value propositions, the copy did not seem to be different enough to see any significant impact on click throughs or donations.

experiments.title: How a Faith and Justice offer impacted the blog subscriptions
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the incentive of Faith and Justice lift the email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2016 - 03/31/2016
In our continued efforts to increase the conversion rate for visitors on the blog, we decided to isolate the Faith and Justice offer to see what impact that would have in visitor conversion. In past experiments, we discovered that Faith and Justice by itself decreased conversion so by adding incremental language to the slide out that offered the subscription we were hoping to see if the added incentive increased the value in the visitors minds.
experiments.keyLearnings: With statistical validity sitting at 73%, we discovered that Faith and Justice did not increase the value of the email acquisition offer in the minds of the visitor. It did not detract from it but we learned that it would not serve as an added incentive for subscription.

experiments.title: How the positioning of the offer affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing the slide out offer in the middle of the blog post increase the email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2016 - 04/20/2016
Our most successful acquisition devices for the ADF blog is currently a slide out offer that appears once the visitor reaches the end of each blog post. The position of the offer was chosen based upon the opinion that people that finish the content would be more likely to convert. We decided to put this hypothesis to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings: Initially, we saw positive results with the slide out offer appearing in the middle of the page but over time, these results diminished until there was no statistical difference. It would appear that there is no major impact between showing the slide out in the middle or at the end of the page.

experiments.title: How value proposition affects visitor motivation on the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will lead to the greatest number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2016 - 06/08/2016
In a recent experiment, we discovered a new value proposition that offered a significant lift to the email acquisition rate on the blog. In prior experiments, we had learned that blog traffic had differing motivations than those coming to the home page. As a result, we decided to test this new value proposition on the home page to see if it would have the same impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant change to email acquisition with the new treatment. This experiment is another reinforcement that the visitors to the blog have very different motivations than those visiting the home page. In this case, the visitors already had a better understanding of what ADF does so the “updates” and “latest news” did not require any additional validation or explanation.

experiments.title: How donor page redesign affects visitor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page will result in the greatest conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2016 - 06/13/2016
After the Safe Bathroom campaign initially launched, we noticed that the donation page had a relatively low conversion rate of 1% to 2%. After examining the donation page, we decided to run a radical redesign that would reduce the length of the page while reinforcing the reason they initially signed the pledge that led them to this page.  This radical redesign would also bring in imagery from the original page to try to reinforce that original value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment is a good example of why it is necessary to give experiments enough time before making any quick decisions. At the initial launch of the experiment, the Control seemed to have a significantly higher conversion rate but, as time went on, this difference leveled out. By the time we ended this experiment, there was less than a 70% level of confidence in either treatment meaning neither showed significant signs of improvement over the other.
This test largely focused on tactical aspects of the page such as form length and imagery. In the end, those changes were not enough to sway the visitors to give a gift.

experiments.title: How the “Next Article” button impacted engagement – V2
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “Next Article” button to the blog increase the percent of people that explore the ADF site?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2016 - 01/03/2017
We had previously validated an experiment that proved a “Next Article” button at the end of a blog actually decreased the amount of people that read the next article. We had doubts about the validity of this experiment due to a technical issue so we decided to run the experiment again to see if the same results would be achieved.
experiments.keyLearnings: This proved what we expected about the previous test; there was a technical issue that had invalidated the results. This experiment proved the simple act of adding the button did not impact the likelihood of the visitor to read the next article.

experiments.title: How reducing visual friction on the donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a donation page with reduced navigation and a smaller donation form increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2017 - 03/20/2017
We conducted a series of experiments on a handful of email list rentals prior to rolling out the message to a wide audience. Once we were confident in the tactic and messaging approach of the appeal, we rolled it out to a larger set of lists.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After the appeal was rolled out to five different email lists, we were still not able to achieve statistical significance with the test treatment. It would seem that the audience had such a high motivation that the navigational elements and form fields were not a significant source of friction.
However, it is worth pointing out that the desktop treatment saw a 14% lift in conversion (not statistically significant) while the mobile treatment was nearly identical to the control. This would seem to indicate that the navigation and form were more of a source of friction for desktop visitors than mobile. This makes sense given that the mobile design already has reduced navigation and the form would be nearly identical due to the responsive design.
To validate this hypothesis, we would need to test this again with a larger audience of desktop visitors.

experiments.title: How a Topical Inline Email Offer Impacts Email Acquisition Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing someone an email acquisition offer that is specific to the article they are on increase the likelihood of conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2017 - 06/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom has identified their blog as a high-traffic section of their site. They noticed, however, that too many mobile visitors were leaving without signing up to receive more Alliance Defending Freedom content. To assist in email acquisition, they decided to add an inline offer for mobile visitors that prompted them to ‘stay informed to changes to your religious freedom,’ by signing up to receive the latest generic updates from Alliance Defending Freedom. After noticing that this inline offer increased email acquisition, they decided to create more specific inline offers for each major topic: Sanctity of life, religious liberty, and marriage and family. They set these treatments up against the more generic control, and documented the results.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test did not validate. However, we can still draw some conclusions from the results. The addition of the specific inline offer did not dramatically increase or decrease email acquisition for mobile visitors. This may indicate that blog visitors were not drawn to one particularly topic but rather Alliance Defending Freedom’s high level value proposition. It may also indicate that the more generic inline offer had a great perceived value (i.e. updates on sanctity of life vs. updates on sanctity of life, religious liberty, and marriage and family). Though not statistically valid, this test gave Alliance Defending Freedom a head start in understanding the motivations and behavior of mobile visitors.

experiments.title: How a ‘Blog Topic’ Survey Impacts User Engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a ‘Blog Topic’ survey increase user engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/07/2017 - 08/11/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom found in a previous test that offering blog readers additional relevant content at the end of the article increased engagement. Since this discovery, they have looked for new ways to (a) further increase engagement and (b) gather feedback on which topics they should cover. With these goals in mind, they launched a ‘Blog Topic’ survey that prompts the reader to select which topic they would like Alliance Defending Freedom to cover next.
After the user made a selection, they were offered one of two different options: additional articles to explore related to their selection or an email acquisition offer. Both of these actions are metrics that we are trying to increase for blog readers so we wanted to see if this style of engagement could impact either one.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither of the two survey options had any significant impact on either of the two tracked metrics: reading more articles and acquiring more emails. The data set above is for the email acquisition rate.
Though this test did not show a statistical difference in the primary metrics, we can draw one conclusion from the results. The ‘Blog Topic’ survey did not have a negative impact on email acquisition or engagement rates. Because of this, we know that Alliance Defending Freedom will be able to gather insightful data from the blog users. This data–collected with no net impact on engagement–can help them determine strategic focuses in the future.

experiments.title: How aligning the content of the email offer to the article impacted acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offers that better relate to the content on the page increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2017 - 06/30/2017
On the mobile version of the Alliance Defending Freedom blog, we wanted to see if we could better align the value of the email offer with the content on the page. The belief was that people reading an article on “Sanctity of Life” or “Culture” would be more likely to respond to an email offer on the same topic.
To do this, we created four different versions of the email acquisition offer targeted at the four categories of blog posts on ADFLegal.org’s blog.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment did not produce any significant lift to the email acquisition rates when it aligned with the content of the article. After running for a full month, it seemed to be negatively impacting the results so we decided to shut it off before it validated. (It wasn’t worth running for another month).
This reinforces something we’ve seen in the past with ADF. Supporters connect with the organizational value proposition of defending religious freedom. Whenever we try to target a specific aspect of that proposition, it negatively impacts results.

experiments.title: How an ‘Up Next’ slideout affects email acquisition rate on Desktop
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase clickthrough rate on desktop by providing a single article that is “Up Next”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2017 - 10/02/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom recently conducted an experiment  on mobile devices, where they asked: Is it possible to increase clickthrough rate by providing an ‘Up Next’ article? The result was a valid 29% increase in traffic without a valid decrease in conversion. They wondered if they would see the same results if they conducted this experiment with desktop users as well. They launched a desktop-only test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom’s experiment did not achieve validity. Though the results were inconclusive, this test may tell us an important thing or two about desktop users. Behavior from one device to another can change significantly. We know that those who view a blog post on a mobile device are already more likely to read more than one article at a time. Due to the differences in motivation and the likely environment of mobile and desktop users, it’s important that we test each separately. Another variable that makes this test different than the mobile-only version is the ‘Up Next’ content the desktop viewer saw. It’s possible this piece of content was less compelling to viewers than what was shown as a treatment in the mobile test.
Further studies are being conducted to determine whether desktop visitors benefit from an ‘Up Next’ slideout or not.

experiments.title: How a case-specific exit intent offers impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an exit intent offer geared towards a particular legal case increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2017 - 10/17/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom was looking for a new way to improve the exit intent offer that visitors see before leaving their site. They wondered if a more specific call-to-action would cause visitors to take action with more urgency. They decided to test an offer around one of their largest legal cases right now: The defense of Jack Phillips. So they created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for over a month, this test did not validate for Alliance Defending Freedom. While they did begin to see a lift with the more case-specific messaging, when compared with the mission-specific messaging (the control), the end results were inconclusive. This has prompted further test ideas regarding the nature and offer of exit intent offers.

experiments.title: How case-specific value proposition copy impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an newsletter signup offer geared towards a particular legal case increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/13/2017 - 10/17/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom recently conducted a test with their exit intent offer, asking: Will a more case-specific offer increase email acquisition? The results of that test were inconclusive but prompted a similar test on their ‘Jack Phillips’ information and email acquisition page. They asked: Will a case-specific newsletter offer increase email acquisition? They created two versions and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Again, Alliance Defending Freedom found that the results were inconclusive, since the test did not reach a statistical significance of 95% and a statistical power of 80%. We would naturally believe that aligning the newsletter offer with the related information on the page would increase email acquisition. And perhaps, it can if given enough time and traffic. However, this test cannot confirm that hypothesis, and thus, requires further testing to prove.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the urgency of a campaign impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will donation page copy that emphasizes the relevance and urgency of Jack’s case increase donor acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2017 - 11/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom was looking for new ways to optimize the performance of their Jack Phillips donation page. They wondered: Can we positively impact visitor motivation and by increasing the relevance and urgency donating to support the Jack Phillip’s case? They created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom found that this test produced no statistically significant results. Therefore, the impact of the treatment version is inconclusive. Still, the fact that this page did not produce a valid result does tell us that the copy used on the treatment page to emphasize the urgency of this case did not increase visitor motivation.

experiments.title: How a banner header image impacts article engagement for desktop users
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the header image on blog pages increase reader engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/26/2017 - 11/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom was interested in knowing how blog page design impacted reader engagement on their site. They wondered: Will removing the header image on blog pages decrease friction and increase reader engagement? They created a trackable goal that measured clicks from one blog to a second article. They then created a treatment version that removed the header image and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom found that the removal of the blog header image did not significantly impact reader engagement on their blog. The treatment page, in other words, did not produce a statistically significant increase or decrease in reader engagement for desktop users.
This test does, however, seek to address an important question–one that further testing will continue to try and answer. That question is: How does design impact page functionality and usability? This is an important, ongoing question that every nonprofit marketer should be motivated to answer for their organization because both form and function must exist in a healthy balance.

experiments.title: How a supporting image affects email acquisition rate on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing a supporting image increase email acquisition rate on a landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2017 - 12/19/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom recently launched a campaign to rally support around Jack Phillips–one of the clients they are defending at the Supreme Court. They found that adding additional copy to their facebook ads did not increase or decrease clickthrough rate. They wondered: Will removing the image of Jack Phillips on the email acquisition page increase the overall email acquisition rate? They created a treatment version (without the image of Jack) and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom found that the the treatment version actually had no statistically significant effect on email acquisition. In other words, we saw no difference in conversion on the page that lacked the supporting image. This is an interesting learning for all of us to consider, as we build new campaign pages. This test suggests that the presence (or lack thereof) of a smaller image doesn’t have a dramatic impact on visitor motivation.
However, it’s also important to note that the conversion rate for this page was much higher than the average acquisition page (converting over 70% of all visitors). Motivation may have been so high, in fact, that it overpowered any page elements that would have otherwise affected page users. Thus, Alliance Defending Freedom will continue to test the image element in order to be certain of its impact.
